name: How to Secure Your Weekly Workers' Comp Checks Without the Stress
description: How to Secure Your Weekly Workers' Comp Checks Without the Stress - Boise Idaho

step.name: Step 1
step.text: If you’ve been injured on the job in Utah or Idaho, you probably expect the process to be straightforward: you get hurt, you report it, and the insurance company pays for your recovery. Unfortunately, the system is designed with multiple 'waiting periods' and technical hurdles that can leave you without a paycheck for weeks.

step.name: Step 2
step.text: Here is exactly how to secure your Temporary Total Disability (TTD) benefits as quickly as possible.

step.name: Step 3
step.text: ### Step 1: Report the Injury Immediately

step.name: Step 4
step.text: In Utah, you technically have 180 days to report an injury, but waiting even a few days can ruin your credibility. If you don't report the injury to a supervisor immediately, the insurance company will argue that you actually got hurt at home over the weekend.

step.name: Step 5
step.text: **Action:** Send a text or email to your boss so there is a time-stamped record of the report.

step.name: Step 6
step.text: ### Step 2: Seek Medical Care and Get a 'Work Status Report'

step.name: Step 7
step.text: You cannot get paid disability benefits just because you feel like you can't work. You need a doctor to say it. At your first appointment, tell the doctor your injury is work-related and ask for a 'Work Status Report' or 'Functional Capacity' form.

step.name: Step 8
step.text: **Action:** Ensure the doctor explicitly checks the box for 'Totally disabled from work' or lists specific 'Light duty restrictions.'

step.name: Step 9
step.text: ### Step 3: Navigate the 3-Day Waiting Period

step.name: Step 10
step.text: In Utah, the law says you aren't paid for the first three days you miss work. This is the 'waiting period.' However, if you are off work for 14 days or more, the insurance company must go back and pay you for those first three days.

step.name: Step 11
step.text: **Action:** Keep a calendar of every day you miss. If you hit day 14, check your next payment to ensure those first three days were included.

step.name: Step 12
step.text: ### Step 4: Verify the Wage Calculation

step.name: Step 13
step.text: Your check should be roughly 66.67% of your 'Average Weekly Wage.' Many adjusters only look at your hourly rate for 40 hours. They often 'forget' to include:

step.name: Step 14
step.text: * Overtime worked in the 6 months prior.

step.name: Step 15
step.text: * Performance or holiday bonuses.

step.name: Step 16
step.text: * Tips and commissions.

step.name: Step 17
step.text: * Income from a second job you can no longer perform.

step.name: Step 18
step.text: **Action:** Gather your paystubs from the last 26 weeks and do the math yourself. If the insurance company is low-balling you, send them the paystubs and demand an adjustment.

step.name: Step 19
step.text: ### Step 5: Address Light-Duty Offers

step.name: Step 20
step.text: If your doctor says you can do 'light duty' (like sitting at a desk) and your boss offers you that job, you must take it. If you refuse a valid light-duty offer, your disability checks will stop immediately.

step.name: Step 21
step.text: **Action:** If the light-duty job pays less than your old job, you are entitled to 'Temporary Partial Disability' to make up the difference. Ensure you are getting paid for that 'wage gap.'

step.name: Step 22
step.text: ### When to Call for Help

step.name: Step 23
step.text: If your checks are more than 14 days late, if the insurance company is ignoring your paystubs, or if they are forcing you back to work before your doctor says you are ready, the 'DIY' phase of your claim is over. Contacting Just Work Comp Law can help you bypass the red tape and get your benefits reinstated.
